output:
# Research Analysis: RADAR -- Reliability-aware, Affinity-Disentangled, Adaptive-depth Relay

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.

The field is graph-based collaborative filtering (graph-neural-network recommenders), whose canonical backbone is LightGCN [1]: it won by deleting complexity, stripping the GCN [6] machinery (feature-transformation matrices and nonlinear activations) that NGCF [2] had inherited, and keeping only neighborhood aggregation plus layer combination over the user-item interaction graph. That minimalism concentrated the entire inductive bias of the model into one load-bearing object -- the propagation operator -- and then froze it. We name the resulting gap Degree-Conflated Uniform Smoothing (DCUS): LightGCN's edge weight 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|) is a fixed, degree-only, content-agnostic, learning-independent scalar that silently fuses three orthogonal quantities -- collaborative affinity (which neighbor is genuinely predictive), the popularity/degree artifact (how many neighbors the endpoints happen to have), and interaction reliability (whether the observed implicit-feedback edge should be trusted at all) -- while the layer-combination weight alpha_k = 1/(K+1) imposes one global smoothing depth on every node, ignoring that a cold-start node is under-smoothed and a hub over-smooths within one or two hops. RADAR replaces the frozen scalar with a learned operator carrying three orthogonal, independently ablatable knobs -- a per-edge affinity weight beta, a per-edge reliability gate g, and a per-node depth profile alpha^(u) -- each driven by signals already present in the embeddings and the interaction graph, and each computed without ever multiplying the propagated embedding by a learned matrix, so RADAR stays strictly inside LightGCN's transform-free regime. The construction is a strict generalization, not a competitor: with the affinity weight set to the degree-normalized constant (beta = 1 under mean-1 renormalization), the reliability gate opened (g = 1), and the depth made uniform (alpha_k^(u) = 1/(K+1)), RADAR recomputes LightGCN within floating-point tolerance, and natural settings of the same knobs recover matrix factorization (K = 0), SGC, APPNP, and a transform-free attention convolution as boundary cases. RADAR therefore contains LightGCN rather than merely beating it: the boundary identities are [provable-by-construction], and every accuracy claim is [conjectured->measured].

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Collaborative filtering (CF) learns latent embeddings for users and items and predicts an interaction by their inner product. The earliest such model, matrix factorization, maps a user/item ID directly to an embedding and is trained for personalized ranking with the Bayesian Personalized Ranking objective (BPR-MF) [17]. A first wave of improvements augmented the user ID with the user's interaction history: SVD++ [18] folded interacted items into the user representation for rating prediction, and NAIS [5] introduced an attention mechanism that differentiates the importance of items in a user's history for item ranking. Viewed through a graph lens, both are one-hop models: they exploit a user's immediate neighbors (her interacted items) but never propagate over higher-hop structure.

The graph-convolution wave deepened that idea. NGCF [2] cast CF as message passing on the user-item bipartite graph, borrowing the full Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) [6] propagation rule -- feature transformation, neighborhood aggregation, and nonlinear activation -- and stacking layers to capture high-hop collaborative signal, reaching state-of-the-art at the time. Related graph recommenders include GC-MC [3], which framed rating prediction as bipartite edge completion, and the web-scale PinSage [4], built on GraphSAGE-style [7] sampled aggregation. The general GNN toolbox these draw on -- GCN [6], GraphSAGE [7], and the attention-based GAT [8] -- supplies the aggregation primitives that CF re-uses.

LightGCN [1] then made the field's defining move: it asked which parts of the GCN machinery actually help CF, and found that feature transformation and nonlinear activation do not -- they make training harder and degrade recommendation accuracy on the interaction graph, where nodes carry only ID embeddings and no input features. LightGCN deletes both, keeping only the linear neighborhood aggregation e_u^(k+1) = sum_{i in N_u} 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|) * e_i^(k) and a layer combination e_u = sum_{k=0}^{K} alpha_k * e_u^(k) with uniform alpha_k = 1/(K+1). The paper's ablations show that symmetric square-root degree normalization on both sides is best, with left-side L1 normalization (the in-degree stochastic matrix) second; symmetry helps the sqrt form but hurts the L1 form. LightGCN further supplies an embedding-smoothness analysis: a 2-layer model smooths a user's embedding toward users who overlap on her interacted items, with a smoothing strength c_{v->u} (Eq. 14 of the source), and the paper argues that this controlled smoothing is the key reason for its effectiveness. The result -- simpler, faster, and stronger than NGCF -- made LightGCN the default backbone that thousands of downstream systems and papers build on.

Three adjacent lines then attacked specific weaknesses of this backbone, each in its own place. The depth line confronts oversmoothing -- the well-documented tendency of stacked graph convolutions to wash node representations toward indistinguishability [11, 12], with normalization remedies such as PairNorm [13] -- via globally fixed depth controllers: SGC [9] collapses propagation to a single precomputed hop, and APPNP [10] replaces stacked convolutions with a fixed geometric personalized-PageRank teleport decay. The reliability line confronts interaction noise: SGL [14] adds self-supervised contrastive learning with graph augmentation and edge dropout, and SimGCL [15] shows that simple embedding-space perturbations suffice, both denoising at the loss level. The popularity line confronts the degree/popularity bias: MACR [16] performs model-agnostic counterfactual reasoning to remove popularity bias at the loss/inference stage. Each of these is a real advance, but each treats one symptom in one location -- architecture (SGC/APPNP), objective (SGL/SimGCL), or inference (MACR) -- while the propagation weight that actually performs the smoothing remains LightGCN's fixed degree-only scalar, and the smoothing depth remains a single global constant. None of them closed the load-bearing gap, DCUS: the operator was never allowed to learn what it was conflating, nor to choose its depth per node.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

**The DCUS confound, made precise.** LightGCN's propagation is governed by exactly two hand-set, learning-independent quantities: the per-edge weight c_{ui} = 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|) and the per-layer weight alpha_k = 1/(K+1). Both are fixed before any embedding is learned and never updated. The edge weight is a pure degree statistic, so it cannot, even in principle, distinguish three quantities that genuinely differ across edges and nodes. We call the conflation Degree-Conflated Uniform Smoothing and decompose it into three orthogonal faces:

- **Face A -- Affinity/Popularity conflation.** Because 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|) depends only on degrees, the operator down-weights every edge incident to a popular item identically, whether or not that particular interaction is informative. It cannot separate an edge carrying genuine collaborative affinity (this user-item pair is mutually predictive) from an edge whose weight is merely a popularity echo. This is the direct source of popularity bias and long-tail starvation.
- **Face B -- Reliability conflation.** Every observed implicit-feedback edge enters propagation with the same full trust, an implicit gate g_{ui} = 1. Accidental clicks, exposure-biased impressions, and missing-not-at-random artifacts smooth as strongly as deliberate, reliable interactions; the graph is treated as clean ground truth.
- **Face C -- Depth uniformity (oversmoothing heterogeneity).** alpha_k = 1/(K+1) applies an identical smoothing depth to a cold-start user (few neighbors, under-smoothed, wanting deeper propagation) and to a hub (many neighbors, over-smoothing within one or two hops). One global depth cannot be correct for nodes whose oversmoothing rates differ by orders of magnitude.

These three faces are orthogonal: affinity is about which neighbor matters, reliability is about whether to trust the edge at all, and depth is about how many hops to combine. Each becomes one independently ablatable RADAR knob.

**The composed adaptive operator.** RADAR replaces the single scalar with a product of three toggleable factors and replaces the global alpha with a per-node simplex weight:

```
Affinity (Knob A):    a_{ui}^(k) = exp( phi(e_u^(k), e_i^(k)) )
                      beta_{ui}^(k) = a_{ui}^(k) / ( sum_{j in N_u} a_{uj}^(k) ) * |N_u|     # mean-1 over neighbors
Reliability (Knob R): g_{ui} = sigmoid( rho( log|N_u|, log|N_i|, interaction-count, embedding-agreement ) ) in [0,1]
Composed edge weight: w_{ui}^(k) = g_{ui} * beta_{ui}^(k) * ( 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|) )
Light propagation:    e_u^(k+1) = sum_{i in N_u} w_{ui}^(k) * e_i^(k)                        # NO transform, NO activation
Depth (Knob D):       alpha^(u) = softmax_k( psi( log|N_u|, per-layer drift ||e_u^(k) - e_u^(k-1)|| ) )
Per-node readout:     e_u = sum_{k=0}^{K} alpha_k^(u) * e_u^(k)
```

The maps phi, rho, psi are tiny: phi is a low-rank bilinear or scalar score (for example <Px, Py>/sqrt(r) with rank r << d, or a learned temperature on cosine), rho is a small logistic map on a handful of scalar edge features, and psi maps node features to a (K+1)-simplex. Crucially, the propagated vector e_i^(k) is never multiplied by a learned matrix, so RADAR introduces no feature transform and stays inside LightGCN's transform-free regime. All three knobs are driven by signals already present in the embeddings and the graph; no side information is required.

**Why mean-1 renormalization makes the collapse exact.** Knob A renormalizes the affinity scores to have mean 1 over each node's neighbors, so beta is a redistribution of the degree-normalized mass rather than an amplification of it: sum_{j in N_u} beta_{uj}^(k) * base = base * |N_u| * mean_j(beta) is conserved. This has two consequences. First, it neutralizes the spectral hazard (Section 3 / Q3.3): an unconstrained learned weight could push the operator's effective gain above 1 and blow up embedding norms across stacked hops -- exactly the instability LightGCN's degree normalization was engineered to prevent -- whereas a mean-1 redistribution cannot inflate total smoothing. Second, it makes the collapse identity exact rather than approximate: when phi is constant, every a_{ui}^(k) is equal, so beta_{ui}^(k) = 1 for all neighbors, and the composed weight reduces to precisely 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|).

**Why naive "just learn the weights" fails.** Three traps make this harder than it looks, and each motivates a specific safeguard. (1) Identifiability: from implicit interactions alone, affinity and reliability are not separately labeled -- a down-weighted edge is observationally consistent with both "low affinity" (Knob A wants it small) and "unreliable" (Knob R wants it small) -- so the orthogonality claim must be demonstrated by an anti-collinearity test, not assumed. (2) Leakage: phi operates on current embeddings, and embedding geometry is itself shaped by degree (popular items drift to high-norm regions during BPR training), so a poorly built phi can learn to re-encode 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|) and deliver LightGCN-in-disguise; this must be refuted by regressing the learned fields on degree features and reporting a non-trivial residual. (3) Scale/oversmoothing instability: replacing the degree norm reintroduces the norm-explosion and norm-collapse risks LightGCN removed, and a mis-learned per-node depth could worsen oversmoothing on a hub; these must be measured against LightGCN's norm trajectories and smoothness statistic.

**The collapse identity as the converting move.** Without an exact collapse, a learned operator is indistinguishable from yet another attention-GNN heuristic -- the kind of added machinery LightGCN's headline result taught the field to distrust. The mean-1 redistribution, the bounded gate, and the per-node depth simplex each toggle independently and all three collapse to LightGCN by construction when off. That identity is what converts a suspicious heuristic into a strict, do-no-harm generalization, and it is a literal unit test (Section 3 / Q5.6, B1) that must recompute LightGCN within floating-point tolerance before any RADAR result is trusted.

## 3. Novel Insights and Recommendations

RADAR should be read as a mechanism-reframing contribution, not a leaderboard entry. The novelty is to name the confound (DCUS), prove the collapse and boundary cases, and then validate disentanglement before claiming any accuracy gain -- the same discipline that distinguishes a measured advance from an over-fit trick.

**First, prove the construction.** The boundary cases are [provable-by-construction] and require no measurement: setting Knob A off (beta = 1), Knob R off (g = 1), and Knob D off (uniform alpha) yields exactly LightGCN; K = 0 yields BPR-MF; alpha_k^(u) = 1[k = K] yields an SGC-flavored readout; alpha_k^(u) = gamma (1-gamma)^k yields an APPNP-flavored decay; Knob A alone (transform-free) yields a GAT-flavored attention convolution; Knob R alone yields an in-operator edge denoiser. These are unit tests, run before any claim.

**Second, validate disentanglement before accuracy.** Because the central risk is that the three knobs are three relabelings of one down-weighting, RADAR must pass attribution gates first: a knob factorial that shows A and R carry separable, non-redundant signal; a leakage test that proves the learned beta and alpha^(u) carry information beyond the degree clock (a non-trivial residual correlated with held-out accuracy); an injected-noise placebo that shows the reliability gate specifically down-weights known accidental edges while preserving genuine long-tail edges (rather than simply pruning the tail); a heterogeneity placebo (random fields matched in marginal distribution) that RADAR must beat; and a capacity-matched LightGCN+ control that rules out "the win is added parameters, not mechanism." Only after these gates does an accuracy claim become meaningful.

**Third, keep every accuracy claim [conjectured->measured].** The targeted effects are local and stratum-specific -- Knob A on popular-item/high-degree edges, Knob R on noisy edges, Knob D on cold-start nodes -- and global Recall@20 / NDCG@20 are head-dominated aggregates that can hide them. The recommendation is therefore to report both the parity metrics and dedicated stratified metrics, and to expect modest global gains alongside larger targeted gains, with do-no-harm non-inferiority on clean/easy settings measured, never guaranteed.

**The transferable pattern.** "Learn and disentangle the propagation weight in-operator, separate affinity from popularity from reliability, and choose smoothing depth per node" generalizes to any GNN that smooths over a graph that is simultaneously noisy, degree-skewed, and heterogeneous in the depth each node needs -- social networks (celebrity hubs vs. periphery), knowledge graphs (high-degree type nodes vs. rare entities), and citation graphs (surveys vs. niche results) all exhibit the same three confounded faces. The oversmoothing literature [11, 12, 13] established that depth is dangerous and heterogeneous but framed the remedy as normalization or a global expressivity bound, not as a per-node, learned, in-operator depth controller co-designed with a reliability gate. RADAR is the concrete demonstration that the three faces are separately ablatable and separately learnable inside a transform-free operator: a reusable design template, with the collapse identity as the safeguard that makes it strictly generalizing.

Here are the five core questions:

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

In graph-based collaborative filtering, can replacing LightGCN's fixed, degree-only, content-agnostic propagation operator -- whose single normalized-adjacency scalar 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|) conflates collaborative affinity, the popularity/degree artifact, and interaction reliability, and whose global uniform layer weights alpha_k = 1/(K+1) impose one smoothing depth on every node -- with an adaptive operator that learns a per-edge affinity weight, a per-edge reliability gate, and per-node layer-combination weights from signals already present in the learned embeddings and the interaction graph, measurably improve recommendation accuracy (Recall@K, NDCG@K) on standard CF benchmarks (Gowalla, Yelp2018, Amazon-Book), while by construction recomputing LightGCN within floating-point tolerance whenever the affinity weight is set to the degree-normalized constant, the reliability gate is opened, and the layer weights are made uniform?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Graph-based collaborative filtering is the workhorse of modern recommenders, and LightGCN [1] is its canonical backbone for a deliberately counterintuitive reason: it won by deleting complexity. Where NGCF [2] inherited the full GCN [6] machinery -- feature transformation matrices and nonlinear activations -- LightGCN's ablations showed those components hurt collaborative filtering, and stripping them down to pure neighborhood aggregation plus layer combination produced a simpler, faster, stronger model. That "deleted complexity is the point" result is exactly why LightGCN matters economically and scientifically: recommenders drive a large fraction of e-commerce, media, and ad revenue, and a backbone that is cheap to train, easy to reason about, and near-state-of-the-art becomes the default that thousands of downstream systems and papers build on. But the very minimalism that made LightGCN canonical also concentrated all of its inductive bias into one load-bearing object -- the propagation operator -- and froze it. The edge weight 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|) is a fixed, degree-only, content-agnostic, learning-independent scalar, and the layer-combination weight alpha_k = 1/(K+1) imposes one global smoothing depth on every node. Both are set before any embedding is learned and never updated. The field has spent years tuning the form of that scalar (sqrt vs. L1, one side vs. two -- LightGCN's own ablation) without ever asking whether the operator's weights should be learned in the first place.

The contribution RADAR offers is best understood as a lens, not a hack. Naming Degree-Conflated Uniform Smoothing (DCUS) asserts that this single frozen scalar silently fuses three orthogonal quantities -- collaborative affinity (which neighbor is genuinely predictive), a popularity/degree artifact (how many neighbors the endpoints happen to have), and interaction reliability (whether the observed implicit-feedback edge should be trusted at all) -- and then applies one depth to nodes whose oversmoothing rates differ by orders of magnitude (a cold-start user wants deeper propagation; a hub over-smooths in one or two hops). Once DCUS is named, a large pile of otherwise-disconnected downstream patches snaps into focus as approximations to one missing capability. SGL [14] and SimGCL [15] fight interaction noise with contrastive denoising and augmentation -- but at the loss level, never inside the propagation weight. MACR [16] and the popularity-debiasing line remove the popularity echo -- but at the loss/inference stage, never by disentangling it from affinity in the operator. APPNP [10] and SGC [9] address depth -- but with a global, fixed decay, never per-node. Each is a community-specific symptom treatment for a single underlying disease: the operator was never allowed to learn what it was conflating. RADAR is the in-operator cure those patches were locally approximating, and it disentangles affinity, popularity, and reliability and chooses depth per node -- from signals the model already computes, with no side information.

The pattern generalizes well beyond CF, which is what makes it interesting to the broader graph-learning community rather than to recommender specialists alone. "Learn and disentangle the propagation weight, in-operator, and make depth per-node" is a recipe for any GNN that smooths over a graph that is simultaneously noisy, degree-skewed, and heterogeneous in the depth each node needs -- social networks (celebrity hubs vs. periphery), knowledge graphs (high-degree type nodes vs. rare entities), and citation graphs (survey papers vs. niche results) all exhibit the same three confounded faces that DCUS names. The oversmoothing literature [11, 12, 13] established that depth is dangerous and heterogeneous, but framed the remedy as normalization (PairNorm) or as a global expressivity bound (Oono and Suzuki), not as a per-node, learned, in-operator depth controller co-designed with a reliability gate. RADAR is the concrete demonstration that the three faces are separately ablatable and separately learnable inside a transform-free operator -- a reusable design template, not a one-dataset trick.

Finally, the practical payoff lands precisely on LightGCN's known weak spots, and the framing's value does not depend on a guaranteed win. Popularity bias and long-tail starvation are direct consequences of Face A (the operator down-weights every edge of a popular item identically, regardless of whether the click was informative); interaction noise and exposure bias are Face B (accidental and missing-not-at-random clicks smooth as strongly as deliberate ones); and the cold-start-vs-hub oversmoothing tension is Face C. RADAR targets each with an independently toggleable knob, and -- because it collapses exactly to LightGCN when the knobs are off (a provable-by-construction identity, recomputed within floating-point tolerance) -- it is a drop-in, strictly generalizing upgrade rather than a competing architecture. That the disentangled weights and per-node depths actually improve Recall@K / NDCG@K on Gowalla / Yelp2018 / Amazon-Book is [conjectured->measured], never asserted; the contribution is to hand the field a measurable axis (and a named confound) it did not previously have. A universal, retraining-free, exactly-collapsing upgrade to the default backbone is the kind of result that itself becomes a default.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The naive reading is "just learn the edge weights and the per-node depth." That is wrong on seven concrete, independent counts. Each is a place a naive approach silently fails, and each maps to a specific design choice and ablation rung in Q5.

1. **Identifiability / disentanglement is underdetermined (the core difficulty).** RADAR's central claim is that one fixed scalar conflates three orthogonal quantities and that knobs A/R/D each recover a distinct one. But from implicit interactions alone these signals are not separately labeled: a single down-weighted edge is observationally consistent with both "low affinity" (Knob A wants it small) and "unreliable/noisy" (Knob R wants it small), and nothing in the raw data says which. The hardness is proving the three knobs capture distinct, non-redundant signal rather than three relabelings of one down-weighting. If A and R are collinear, RADAR has three parameter groups doing one job and per-knob attribution is meaningless. This demands an explicit anti-collinearity test (the knob-pair factorial plus a within-strata decorrelation check on the learned beta and g fields). It is [designed] that the knobs are architecturally orthogonal (A redistributes mass over a fixed node's neighbors, R is a per-edge scalar gate, D is a per-node simplex over depth); it is [conjectured->measured] that they carry separable signal on real data.

2. **Leakage / collapse-in-disguise: the affinity score can secretly re-encode degree.** phi operates on current embeddings, and embedding norms and geometry are themselves shaped by degree (popular items drift to high-norm regions during BPR training). A poorly built phi can therefore learn to output a score that, after mean-1 renormalization, just re-encodes 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|) -- so beta silently tracks the degree statistic it was meant to disentangle from. If it does, RADAR claims adaptivity but delivers LightGCN, and any headline gain comes from incidental capacity. The hardness is proving beta (and alpha^(u)) carry information beyond the degree clock; this is what the Leakage rung exists to test, by regressing the learned fields on degree features and reporting a region-specific residual correlated with held-out accuracy.

3. **Scale preservation and training stability across K hops.** LightGCN's degree norm is not cosmetic: it keeps the spectral radius of the propagation operator bounded so embedding norms neither blow up nor vanish across K stacked hops and the BPR gradient stays well-conditioned. A learned multiplicative weight reintroduces exactly that instability -- an unconstrained beta can push the operator's effective gain above 1 and explode norms, and an over-eager gate g -> 0 can collapse norms and create dead edges -- during training, where it also destabilizes the gradient. The mean-1 renormalization of beta is designed to neutralize this (it makes Knob A a pure redistribution that conserves total smoothing), and the gate g in [0,1] is strictly contractive, but "designed not to destabilize" must be shown by measuring embedding-norm trajectories, an oversmoothness statistic, and training-loss stability against LightGCN.

4. **The reliability gate has no ground truth.** Knob R must decide whether an edge is genuine collaborative signal or an accidental click / exposure-biased impression / missing-not-at-random artifact, but implicit feedback ships with no labels for "accidental." The gate must be learned self-supervised from indirect cues, with the ever-present risk that it learns the wrong invariant: a gate trained only to improve aggregate Recall has every incentive to suppress genuine long-tail / cold-start edges (individually low-signal, high-variance) -- the very edges RADAR exists to protect. So "the gate improves accuracy" and "the gate is doing reliability filtering, not tail pruning" are distinct claims the data cannot adjudicate on its own, which is why the synthetic injected-noise placebo (where we do have labels) is mandatory: the gate must specifically down-weight the injected edges while preserving genuine tail edges, or the reliability axis is inert regardless of any Recall movement.

5. **Per-node depth is data-hungry exactly where it is needed: cold-start chicken-and-egg.** Knob D's whole motivation is that cold-start nodes are under-smoothed and want deeper propagation while hubs over-smooth and want shallower -- but the nodes that most need a custom depth profile (cold-start users/items with a handful of interactions) are precisely those with the least data to estimate it. A free per-node depth parameter fit from 2-3 edges would overfit catastrophically. The designed fix is amortization: psi is a tiny shared map over node features (log|N_u|, per-layer drift), with no free per-node parameters, so a cold-start node inherits a depth profile from the population function evaluated at its low degree and slow-converging drift, rather than estimating one from scratch. That only works if those features are actually predictive of the right depth for unseen low-degree nodes -- an empirical claim tested in the cold-start Recovery rung.

6. **Cost / parameter parity: RADAR must not win merely by adding capacity.** Any added parameters or compute can buy a small Recall bump independent of the mechanism, so a RADAR-beats-LightGCN result is confounded by capacity unless controlled. The difficulty is to deliver the three knobs parameter-cheap and compute-cheap (phi a low-rank/scalar score, rho a tiny logistic map, psi a small map to a (K+1)-simplex -- collectively a negligible fraction of the ID-embedding budget that dominates CF, adding only a per-edge scalar multiply per hop), and then to make all comparisons parameter- and compute-matched, including a capacity-matched LightGCN+ control given the same extra budget RADAR spends. If RADAR does not beat that control, the win is capacity, not mechanism.

7. **The targeted effects are heterogeneous and standard metrics cannot see them.** RADAR's three promises are local: Knob A should help on popular-item/high-degree edges, Knob R on noisy edges, Knob D on cold-start nodes. But Recall@20 / NDCG@20 are global aggregates dominated by the head. A method could lift tail / cold-start / noise-robustness substantially while moving global Recall@20 by a rounding error, or nudge global Recall with zero effect on any targeted stratum -- so evaluating on global metrics alone cannot attribute, or even detect, the targeted contribution. The remedy is fit-for-purpose stratified metrics (long-tail Recall and Gini, accuracy under injected noise, cold-start-user Recall, an oversmoothing measure reusing LightGCN's Eq. 14 smoothness, and a parameter/time pair); reusing global metrics is a methodological trap that would make a real contribution look null.

**Bottom line.** The difficulty is the conjunction of an underdetermined disentanglement, a leakage trap that can deliver LightGCN-in-disguise, scale/stability hazards from removing the degree norm, a label-free reliability gate that can suppress the signal it should protect, a cold-start chicken-and-egg, a capacity confound, and heterogeneous targeted effects invisible to global metrics. None is incidental; each is precisely why the obvious baseline silently fails, and each maps to a specific design choice and ablation rung.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Every adjacent literature made an assumption that hid the seam, so the unclaimed cell was never even an object of study. NGCF [2] re-added the feature transforms and nonlinearities that LightGCN [1] later showed hurt CF -- it optimized along the wrong axis entirely (more transform capacity), leaving the edge weight itself a fixed degree-norm. The attention-GNN line -- GAT [8], with GraphSAGE [7] and PinSage [4] as its web-scale cousins -- does learn edge weights, but it carries the transforms and nonlinearities LightGCN deleted (so it is off the transform-free spine by construction), it keeps depth global, and, critically, its single attention score cannot zero a noisy edge as distinct from a low-affinity one: attention answers "which neighbor matters," conflating "this edge is informative" with "this edge is trustworthy" back into one number -- re-committing two of the three DCUS faces it was supposed to separate. SGC [9] and APPNP [10] confront depth directly, but fix it globally -- SGC collapses to the final hop, APPNP imposes a single geometric teleport decay -- never per-node, and their edge weights stay frozen. SGL [14] and SimGCL [15] confront reliability, but as loss-level denoising: graph augmentation, edge dropout, and contrastive regularization sit outside the operator, so the propagation weight that actually does the smoothing is still the fixed degree-norm. MACR [16] confronts the popularity echo, but as loss/inference-level counterfactual reasoning, never by separating popularity from affinity inside the propagation weight. And the one-hop neighborhood models -- SVD++ [18] and NAIS [5] -- weight interacted items but do no high-hop graph propagation at all, so depth and reliability never arise as questions. Each community solved one face, in its own preferred place (loss, conditioning, architecture, inference), and none solved it in the operator, disentangled into three axes, per-node.

The closest near-misses sharpen the wedge. GAT [8] is the nearest in spirit -- it learns per-edge weights -- but the precise differentiation is threefold and exact: GAT (i) keeps the transforms/nonlinearities that put it off LightGCN's spine, (ii) has no reliability gate able to gate out an unreliable edge separately from a low-affinity one, and (iii) keeps depth global; it also does not collapse to LightGCN. APPNP [10] is the nearest on the depth axis, but its decay is a fixed global hyperparameter, not a learned per-node simplex over layers driven by each node's own per-layer drift. SGL [14] and SimGCL [15] are nearest on the reliability axis, but they denoise the graph and the objective, not the weight; RADAR's reliability gate g_{ui} in [0,1] is a deterministic, in-operator analogue of their edge-dropout, without the contrastive scaffolding. No prior method disentangles affinity / popularity / reliability inside LightGCN's transform-free edge weight AND makes depth per-node AND recovers LightGCN exactly. That triple conjunction -- three orthogonal, independently ablatable knobs co-located in one transform-free operator with a provable collapse identity -- is the cell none of them touch.

Two barriers kept it unsolved, one conceptual and one structural. Conceptually, the operator was treated as a fixed normalization to be hand-tuned, not as a function to be learned. The subfield's mental model was "pick the right normalization form" -- a low-dimensional design search over sqrt-vs-L1 and one-side-vs-two -- under which "learn and disentangle the weight" is not a harder version of the same question but a different question the normalization frame cannot even pose; spatial heterogeneity across edges and depth heterogeneity across nodes were literally unrepresentable while the operator was a scalar template. Structurally, without the exact collapse identity, a learned operator looks like just another attention-GNN heuristic -- NGCF or GAT with extra parts -- and the field had already learned (from LightGCN's own headline result) to distrust added machinery on the CF graph. The contribution that unlocks the cell is therefore not merely "make the weight learnable" but the constrained construction: a mean-1 renormalized affinity redistribution (so it cannot inflate total smoothing), a separate reliability gate, and a per-node depth simplex, each toggling independently and all three collapsing to LightGCN by construction when off. The collapse identity is what converts a suspicious attention heuristic into a strict, do-no-harm generalization -- and it is exactly the move no prior work made, because each was committed to its own axis (transforms, attention, global depth, loss-level denoising, inference-level debiasing) rather than to the transform-free operator's frozen weight itself.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

### Method

RADAR keeps LightGCN's parameterization (ID embeddings only) and BPR objective and makes only the aggregation weights and the layer-combination depth adaptive; the propagated embedding is never multiplied by a learned matrix (transform-free). End to end, for one model:

1. **Embedding init.** User/item ID embeddings E^(0) (Xavier/normal), exactly as LightGCN. No feature transforms anywhere downstream.
2. **Per-layer affinity (Knob A).** At hop k, score each edge a_{ui}^(k) = exp(phi(e_u^(k), e_i^(k))), where phi is a tiny low-rank bilinear / scalar map (phi(x,y) = <Px, Py>/sqrt(r) with rank r << d, or a learned temperature on cosine); no matrix multiplies the propagated vector. Renormalize mean-1 over each node's neighbors: beta_{ui}^(k) = a_{ui}^(k) / (sum_{j in N_u} a_{uj}^(k)) * |N_u|, so Knob A redistributes the degree-normalized mass without inflating it (stability; exact B1 collapse).
3. **Per-edge reliability gate (Knob R).** g_{ui} = sigmoid(rho(log|N_u|, log|N_i|, interaction count, embedding agreement)), with rho a tiny logistic map; g_{ui} in [0,1] (strictly contractive, safe by construction). Computed once or per epoch, shared across hops.
4. **Composed light propagation.** w_{ui}^(k) = g_{ui} * beta_{ui}^(k) * (1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|)) and e_u^(k+1) = sum_{i in N_u} w_{ui}^(k) * e_i^(k) -- reliability x affinity x degree-norm base; NO transform, NO activation.
5. **Per-node adaptive depth (Knob D).** alpha^(u) = softmax_k(psi(log|N_u|, per-layer drift ||e_u^(k) - e_u^(k-1)||)) over k = 0..K (psi a tiny shared map, no free per-node params, amortized for cold-start); final e_u = sum_{k=0}^{K} alpha_k^(u) * e_u^(k).
6. **Loss.** Standard BPR on y_hat_{ui} = e_u * e_i (same negatives/sampling as LightGCN), plus L2 on E^(0) and a small L2 on the phi/rho/psi params; no auxiliary contrastive loss (RADAR puts denoising inside the operator, distinguishing it from SGL/SimGCL).
7. **Do-no-harm gate (global, MEASURED).** s = 1[Var_edges(w_{ui}/(1/sqrt(.))) > nu_w OR Var_u(alpha^(u)) > nu_alpha]. If not s (signals uninformative): set beta <- 1, g <- 1, alpha_k^(u) <- 1/(K+1), giving exactly LightGCN [provable-by-construction]. Non-inferiority within epsilon is MEASURED, never guaranteed.
8. **Inference.** Identical forward pass (estimators are cheap scalar maps); top-K recommendation by e_u * e_i, exactly LightGCN's inference path.

### Datasets and models

Backbones: LightGCN as the primary backbone RADAR upgrades, plus a drop-in onto a second light propagation backbone (for example LR-GCCF, or the SGL/SimGCL encoder with its contrastive loss ablated on/off) to show RADAR is a propagation-operator upgrade rather than a LightGCN-specific hack. Datasets: Gowalla, Yelp2018, and Amazon-Book using LightGCN's own published splits and preprocessing (same train/test files, same 10-core filtering, same negative sampling), reproducing LightGCN's reported numbers within reproduction tolerance first as a gate before any RADAR claim. A long-tail / cold-start stratification is derived from these (partition test users by interaction-count quantiles, items by popularity quantiles) with no retraining. A synthetic injected-noise variant adds a controlled fraction (5/10/20%) of random/accidental edges with known identity -- the only setting where reliability has ground truth, enabling the Oracle gate and the injected-noise placebo.

### Baselines

All at equal embedding dimension, equal depth K, and equal training budget, with parameter count and wall-clock training time reported (parity is a first-class metric):
- **BPR-MF** [17] -- no propagation (= RADAR with K = 0; Boundary case 2).
- **NGCF** [2] -- adds the transforms LightGCN deleted; fixed degree-norm edge weight.
- **LightGCN** [1] -- PRIMARY baseline (= RADAR's A-off / R-open / D-uniform corner; Boundary case 1, must recompute within floating-point tolerance).
- **SGL** [14], **SimGCL** [15] -- self-supervised / contrastive denoisers (head-to-head on the noise stratum; RADAR's reliability axis lives inside the operator instead of in the loss).
- **GAT-conv-CF** [8] -- attention edge weights with transforms/nonlinearity (RADAR's A-only corner is the transform-free analogue, isolating the value of dropping the transform).
- **APPNP-CF** [10] -- fixed geometric-decay depth (isolates per-node-learned vs. fixed-global depth).
- **Capacity-matched LightGCN+** -- LightGCN given the same extra parameter/FLOP budget RADAR spends (wider dim or more epochs); RADAR must beat this, else the win is capacity, not mechanism.
- **Placebo** -- random per-edge weights / per-node depth matched in marginal distribution to RADAR's learned fields (controls for "any heterogeneity helps").

### Metrics

Parity suite (match LightGCN exactly): Recall@20 and NDCG@20, same K, same all-ranking protocol. Targeted metrics that make the heterogeneous effects visible: (1) long-tail / tail-item Recall plus item-coverage and the Gini coefficient of recommendation frequency (popularity axis -> Knob A); (2) noise-robustness -- Recall@20 / NDCG@20 under the injected-noise variant at each noise level, and accuracy retention vs. LightGCN as noise rises (reliability axis -> Knob R); (3) cold-start-user Recall on the bottom-decile users (depth axis -> Knob D); (4) parameter count and training time (parity); (5) embedding smoothness / oversmoothing, reusing LightGCN's own smoothness definition (Eq. 14, c_{v->u} smoothing strength) to test whether learned depth/weights reduce pathological oversmoothing on hubs while deepening cold-start propagation; (6) gate-transparency and long-tail preservation (a responsible-AI metric): report the distribution of the learned reliability gate g_{ui} stratified by item-popularity quartile and user cold-start decile, together with a named preserve-genuine-long-tail-edges rate (the fraction of tail / cold-start edges that survive the gate at a fixed operating point), so the gate is audited for disparate impact rather than assumed benign. Because RADAR deliberately sharpens collaborative affinity, item-coverage and the Gini coefficient above are reported as an explicit exposure-diversity / filter-bubble commitment -- a first-class reported axis, not an incidental statistic -- to verify that accuracy gains are not purchased by narrowing exposure or starving long-tail and minority-group items.

### Ablations

**Boundary-case sanity checks [provable-by-construction]** (recover by construction, no measurement -- verifying the construction before any claim):
- **B1 -- LightGCN** (phi const so beta = 1; g = 1; alpha_k^(u) = 1/(K+1)): must recompute LightGCN within floating-point tolerance -- a CI unit test; if it fails, the implementation is wrong before any result is trusted.
- **MF / BPR-MF** (K = 0), **SGC-flavored** (alpha = 1[k = K], A/R off), **APPNP-flavored** (alpha_k = gamma (1-gamma)^k, A/R off), **GAT-flavored** (A on, R/D off, transform-free attention conv), **operator-denoiser** (R on, A/D off). Each reproduces its named regime by construction.

**Knob factorial (orthogonality, the direct test of difficulty 1):** run one-at-a-time, all pairs, and all-three across the {A off/on} x {R off/on} x {D uniform/adaptive} cube. The A-off / R-off / D-uniform corner equals LightGCN; the all-on corner is full RADAR. A and R together must beat both A-only and R-only by more than additive noise, and the learned beta and g fields must be decorrelated within strata, or A and R are collinear and the disentanglement claim fails.

**Four diagnostic rungs:**
- **Oracle (upper bound on R).** On the injected-noise variant, gate edges using the known injected-noise labels (perfect g). This is the ceiling on what the reliability axis can buy and tells us how much of that headroom the learned, label-free gate captures. An oracle that uses test-time labels is an upper bound only, never a deployable result.
- **Placebo (heterogeneity control).** Random per-edge weights / per-node depth matched in marginal distribution to RADAR's learned fields. RADAR must beat Placebo, in two legs: (i) structure-destroying (shuffle the learned fields across edges/nodes), (ii) absent-mechanism (a clean dataset where noise/popularity/cold-start pressure is minimal, so the RADAR-vs-LightGCN gap should be approximately zero).
- **Recovery (stratum-specific causal check).** On the subsets where LightGCN is weakest -- popularity-biased, injected-noise, cold-start -- does the relevant knob specifically help? A on the popular-item stratum, R on the injected-noise stratum, D on the cold-start stratum. A knob that helps globally but not on its target stratum is winning for the wrong reason.
- **Leakage (anti-degree-clock test).** Regress the learned beta_{ui} and alpha^(u) on degree features (log|N_u|, log|N_i|, raw 1/sqrt(|N_u| |N_i|)); report the region-specific residual and its correlation with held-out per-edge / per-node accuracy. A non-trivial residual proves the operator is not the degree clock in disguise; if beta is near-perfectly explained by degree, RADAR is LightGCN-with-extra-params and the contribution collapses.

### Expected outcomes

All numbers are measured targets, not guarantees. On the standard splits, modest Recall@20 / NDCG@20 gains over LightGCN (head-dominated metric -- small movement expected and honest). On the targeted strata, larger gains: A on long-tail Recall and Gini, R on injected-noise robustness, D on cold-start Recall and the oversmoothing statistic on hubs. Per-knob attribution: A specifically improves the popularity/tail stratum and lowers Gini; R specifically improves injected-noise robustness and identifies injected edges above chance; D specifically improves cold-start-user Recall. Do-no-harm: on easy/clean settings (signals uninformative, gate fires), RADAR is within epsilon of LightGCN on every metric (measured non-inferiority). Attribution gates: Placebo < RADAR (signal, not heterogeneity); Leakage residual non-trivial (not the degree clock); Recovery shows each knob rescues its target stratum; RADAR beats the capacity-matched LightGCN+ (mechanism, not capacity); Oracle >= RADAR >= LightGCN on the noise variant (RADAR captures a measurable fraction of the reliability ceiling).

**Q1<->Q5 consistency.** Q5 delivers exactly Q1's promise and no more. The collapse to LightGCN and all boundary cases are [provable-by-construction], verified by the B1 floating-point-tolerance unit test and the boundary rungs. The accuracy improvements on standard CF benchmarks, the per-knob disentanglement, and the do-no-harm non-inferiority are [conjectured->measured], delivered by the metrics, knob factorial, Leakage/Placebo/Recovery/Oracle rungs, and capacity-matched control. The epistemic strength of every Q5 outcome is at most the matching Q1 claim: RADAR claims a construction (provable) plus measured effects (conjectured), and never a guarantee of improvement.
